words: This conversation with TOM WAHL of Genesee, Idaho, took place at his home in
Pullman, Washington on March 10, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.
THOMAS WAHL: One of my earliest knowledge of what the Genesee area was like
originally, is pretty well described by my father speaking of riding
from our place, six miles east of Genessee, to Lewiston on a saddle
horse, without crossing a fence, and with the bunchgrass rubbing the
horse's belly all the way down to the lower part of the Lewiston Can
yon. This is something that I came too late to see. I was born in
1911. So my_ memory of that country, of course, is all fences and
plowed ground and mud roads. Mud roads were a thing that very few
people now experience, unless they make a wrong turn in the spring
or the fall in our area and leave the gravelled road, then find out
what mud roads are. I was on the end of a mud road six miles away
from the nearest town. And for certain stages of the year in this
country, that's isolation. Your trips to town were infrequent
enough that you considered that quite an event, when you were a child
in those days. There was one period of the year when you got to go
to town often enough that it could have become monotonous, were it
not for this contrast of the long period of isolation. The time you
got to go to town was when the crops were hauled into town and de
livered. Then you got to go every day, or maybe twice a day. And
this was for a boy that was big enough to enjoy it and too small to
work. This was quite an enjoyable thing.

speaker: SS
words: You'd just go in with your father on the wagon, then?

speaker: TW
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Ride in and back?

speaker: TW
words: Along with this same type of thing, you must realize that all of the

speaker: TW
words: heating of our homes for the winter was accomplished by burning wood.

speaker: TW
words: And in the Genesee area there never was any great amount of wood

speaker: TW
words: very close. Some of the canyons had trees in; frequently some of

speaker: TW
words: this was used as fuel. And, in my day, it was supplemented somewhat

speaker: TW
words: if you had accumulated a big enough woodpile it would be supplemented

speaker: TW
words: sometimes by buying coal in the nearest town. This coal, you

speaker: TW
words: can see almost had to be hauled before the roads deteriorated to the

speaker: TW
words: point where it became a pretty difficult thing. So, after the har

speaker: TW
words: vest, one of the last things you did in the fall was to try to ac

speaker: TW
words: cumulate enough wood to see you through another year of farming. You

speaker: TW
words: might think of this as a hardship, but really this was like a vaca

speaker: TW
words: tion; we got our wood from Troy, or north of Troy, or near Troy. And

speaker: TW
words: one of the places where we frequently got the wood was from OK Olson's

speaker: TW
words: sawmill, which was within, what would you say, a mile or two of Troy?

speaker: SS
words: I'd say about three miles.

speaker: TW
words: Three miles of Troy. And we would haul this home on a mud road, re

speaker: TW
words: member. By this time the road was either dry and dusty or it would

speaker: TW
words: be a time when the road was frozen.hard. And we would leave our farm

speaker: TW
words: in the morning, and we'd go up to— first in the direction of Julietta

speaker: TW
words: and then take the the first left-hand road up a canyon that drains in

speaker: TW
words: to the Little Potlatch Creek. This road turned to the left before

speaker: TW
words: you reached the Little Potlatch Creek; there is another road roughly

speaker: TW
words: paralleling it that is about a mile or so further down when you turn

speaker: TW
words: off on it. And we would get up to the place where we loaded our wood.

speaker: TW
words: Usually OK Olson's sawmill, and we would get there in time to load a

speaker: TW
words: load up that night and we would cook our dinner and sleep overnight

speaker: TW
words: in our sleeping bags, or before my time, of course, the pioneers

speaker: TW
words: didn't have sleeping bags, it was more conventional bedding.

speaker: SS
words: Would you stay right there at the mill?

speaker: TW
words: Yes. And I must say here that the OK Olson family was a most hos

speaker: TW
words: pitable family. We had never been there but what we were invited

speaker: TW
words: to share their meal with them. But, I can only recall one time that

speaker: TW
words: we did this, because, contrary to what you might think, pioneers

speaker: TW
words: were well aware of the trouble that another family might be put to

speaker: TW
words: when they're already feeding a large number of people to stretch the

speaker: TW
words: table for two more. And the next morning, we would start out for

speaker: TW
words: home and get down at the farm in time to unload this load of wood.

speaker: TW
words: That was the routine.

speaker: SS
words: How much wood do you think you would get in a load?

speaker: TW
words: Well, I was making these trips with my father, I believe the first

speaker: TW
words: one I made that stands out in my memory, I was big enough to drive a

speaker: TW
words: team with two horses pulling the wagon. And he drove a team with

speaker: TW
words: four horses pulling his wagon. And, I presume that the two of us,

speaker: TW
words: and this is just a real wild guess, but I presume that the two of us

speaker: TW
words: on the two wagons would haul more than a cord of wood. As I say,

speaker: TW
words: this wasn't a hardship, this was a vacation, like a camping trip. Ev

speaker: TW
words: erything you saw was new. I was going to describe the route we

speaker: TW
words: took; we would go up this road, up this canyon, across a little

speaker: TW
words: divide, down into a second canyon and we would probably go through,

speaker: TW
words: or near to, - well, we went through what was then called iville,

speaker: TW
words: which was a little settlement. There was a store and post office

speaker: TW
words: and schoolhouse. And Cornwall, near what we speak of Joel now, and

speaker: TW
words: from there on into Troy and up the other side a ways til we came to

speaker: TW
words: the sawmill.

speaker: SS
words: Just one trip a year or would this have taken more than one to—?

speaker: TW
words: No, it would take several trips like this to get a year's supply of

speaker: TW
words: wood. I can't remember how much wood it required, and it varied,

speaker: TW
words: too, it would depend on the year. We usually would keep a backlog of

speaker: TW
words: some sort of fuel there and there was always other sources of fuel,

speaker: TW
words: and if the weather wasn't suitable, you might not get to make this

speaker: TW
words: trip at all. You might have to burn refuse and what you had left

speaker: TW
words: and there was, too, in those days, remember, one method ot taking up

speaker: TW
words: land by means of a timber claim, wherein you planted trees, and these

speaker: TW
words: trees, as a general rule were pretty worthless type of a tree, and

speaker: TW
words: they no more than really come to an appreciable size before the pro

speaker: TW
words: cess of grubbing out these timber claims— timbers— began. So,

speaker: TW
words: they served a purpose of supplying some alternate fuel and we relied

speaker: TW
words: on them frequently. And as I have said, you did supplement it with

speaker: TW
words: coal, and there were always the deteriorating fences with the result

speaker: TW
words: ing fenceposts and so forth that had to be replaced, and what remain

speaker: TW
words: ed of the old fenceposts could be burned.

speaker: SS
words: How long could you anticipate that you would be perhaps socked in, is

speaker: SS
words: olated during the winter months?

speaker: TW
words: If we happened to have a muddy fall, the thing we looked to— looked

speaker: TW
words: forward to with a great deal of anticipation was the good sleighingwould

speaker: TW
words: be a year with good sledding. Sometimes it was and sometimes

speaker: TW
words: it wasn't. But this was a vast improvement — snow covered roads

speaker: TW
words: a vast improvement for a horsedrawn economy over a muddy road. There

speaker: TW
words: are instances where a strong team of horses can't even move a wagon

speaker: TW
words: that's stuck in the mud. Not only a strong team of horses, two or

speaker: TW
words: three teams of horses. You have no idea of how deep the mud in this

speaker: TW
words: country was, particularly in an area where it was traveled or in a

speaker: TW
words: low area where the water didn't drain off. I've heard my mother tell

speaker: TW
words: of a team— or rather— of a wagon that was stuck in the main street

speaker: TW
words: of Moscow for a long period of time. I don't believe I would be

speaker: TW
words: misquoting her, if I said all winter. Of course, Moscow, and the

speaker: TW
words: towns were the first settlements to pave certain streets. And my

speaker: TW
words: memory doesn't go back to the days of mud streets in Main Street of

speaker: TW
words: Iioscow, although I have been stuck with a Model T within two blocks

speaker: TW
words: of Main Street. But that was as late as 1930- '31. Mud roads were

speaker: TW
words: pretty plentiful here in Pullman when I came here in 1945. I don't

speaker: TW
words: think you'd have to hunt too far to find mud roads yet, if you looked

speaker: TW
words: in suitable parts.

speaker: SS
words: No. Ours is kind of muddy. We live near the old O.K. Olson place.

speaker: TW
words: Oh, you do?

speaker: SS
words: Going through that canyon at this time of year, it's pretty muddy

speaker: SS
words: sometimes.

speaker: TW
words: There is a thing that would interest you a little bit— but in gener

speaker: TW
words: al— what we called the mountain roads, the mountain country, therein

speaker: TW
words: general the road as muddy, that is, the mud isn t as muddy

speaker: TW
words: as the loam and clay soil that most represents a great deal of the

speaker: TW
words: Palouse. Those mountain areas where there: is quite a bit of de

speaker: TW
words: composed granite in the soil didn't ever get as bad as the lower roads

speaker: TW
words: that I speak of, except in spots.

speaker: SS
words: Let me ask you a little bit more about this isolation— winter isola

speaker: SS
words: tion, especially. But isolation in general. What did you do to pass

speaker: SS
words: the time in those months?

speaker: TW
words: Your question is a question I would expect from someone who hasn't ex

speaker: TW
words: perienced much of the horsepowered era. And what did we do? We tried

speaker: TW
words: to live. And that means, we had to care for our animals. We had to

speaker: TW
words: feed our horses. In order to live, we had to have a source of food,

speaker: TW
words: and a large paftof this was supplied by cattle; meat and milk. And,

speaker: TW
words: also, chickens and fowls of other types. So, all these things have

speaker: TW
words: to be cared for in the winter. You have to either have stockpiled or

speaker: TW
words: be able to get this food— animal food- when needed it. And you

speaker: TW
words: had to distribute it to them every day. Now usually, the horses

speaker: TW
words: ran out without shelter and were fed at least once or maybe twice a

speaker: TW
words: day. Horses and cattle, both. And then, the cows that you were milk

speaker: TW
words: ing and any animals that needed care were stabled in the barn. It

speaker: TW
words: wasn't considered economical to try to put every animal you had in

speaker: TW
words: the barn. In the first place, they wouldn't have survived as well,

speaker: TW
words: and in the second place, you didn't want to use this hay that was un

speaker: TW
words: der aroof, because there wasn't enough of it. You were limited in

speaker: TW
words: jootA the amount of hay under aroof. But^hay, properly stacked, keeps

speaker: TW
words: very well out in ahaystack. And so, during the haying season, June

speaker: TW
words: and July, you put up all the hay that you could put up, and there

speaker: TW
words: was quite a lot of it put up; people raised alfalfa, and most all

speaker: TW
words: of the flat areas that are now plowed were, in those days, in timothy,

speaker: TW
words: timothy flax. Timothy is an excellent horse food. So a lot of our

speaker: TW
words: time in the summer was spent in providing things that we had to have

speaker: TW
words: for use in the winter. And the isolated periods, we were kept real

speaker: TW
words: busy, and incidentally, entertained by surviving, by caring for these

speaker: TW
words: stock and so forth. We had to sort our pile of potatoes over several

speaker: TW
words: times. We had to sort our apples out and discard those that were rot

speaker: TW
words: ting. We usually had carrots, turnips, rutabags, as well as potatoes.

speaker: TW
words: Whatever we could have harvested from our garden and from our farm,

speaker: TW
words: why, these we tried to preserve as well as we could along through

speaker: TW
words: the winter. It was a different type of life from that which we have

speaker: TW
words: now. Considerably easier now, but we have lost some things, too, by

speaker: TW
words: it. Now, we work more indirectly at our survival— but don't get me

speaker: TW
words: off on this, I do a lot of philosophizing about this and I haven't

speaker: TW
words: organized it so that I can talk very coherently.

speaker: SS
words: That sounds like an interesting contrast that you were just beginning

speaker: SS
words: to make. I think it's true. We do work indirectly, certainly com

speaker: SS
words: pared to what you are describing. That seems very directly related

speaker: SS
words: to immediate needs and having enough to eat.

speaker: TW
words: Yes. Putting out your hand and eating implies a whole lot more than

speaker: TW
words: the simplicity of the statement might lead one to believe. But, at

speaker: TW
words: least, in this kind of a life, you don't have to be very old before

speaker: TW
words: you see the necessity of what you are doing. And, although, the work

speaker: TW
words: is hard, it becomes a thing you want to do, because it means comfort.

speaker: TW
words: One example of this: When this was first called to my attention—

speaker: TW
words: in hauling this wood for fuel for the winter, although we regarded it

speaker: TW
words: as a vacation, one part of this that was not a vacation, was loading

speaker: TW
words: and unloading these loads of wood as fast as you could because the

speaker: TW
words: theory being you could rest while you were riding back to get the

speaker: TW
words: next load, and I said— I was protesting in somewhat illhumor at my

speaker: TW
words: father at this hard work of unloading this wood, and I think I ex

speaker: TW
words: pressed a dislike for wood— and his answer to me was, "That wood

speaker: TW
words: will look pretty good to you this winter when you are toasting your

speaker: TW
words: shins by it." So, you weren't very old, because you began, I mean

speaker: TW
words: before you began to like to do what you were forced to do.

speaker: SS
words: The description of the things you were working on suggests a little

speaker: SS
words: more diversification than I thought might be usual case of the farm-

speaker: SS
words: ers on the Palouse at that period of time. It sounds like you were

speaker: SS
words: involved in selfsufficiency to quite a degree.

speaker: TW
words: Yes. There is a little parallel there that would interest you. Now,

speaker: TW
words: a farmer in the wintertime should. whether he does or not, he should

speaker: TW
words: be overhauling and repairing his tractor and his machinery for the

speaker: TW
words: next season's operation. Most of them do. In those days, you were

speaker: TW
words: caring for your horses, oiling your harness, repairing it. There is

speaker: TW
words: a thing that might not occur to someone who hasn't experienced itis

speaker: TW
words: the amount of labor involved in keeping the harnesses, that are

speaker: TW
words: necessary when you use horses as a source of power— in keeping these

speaker: TW
words: harnesses in repair. In those days our breakdowns usually consisted

speaker: TW
words: of either the machinery you were pulling, or the rigging that you used

speaker: TW
words: to pull it with, and we made singletrees and doubletrees and singletree

speaker: TW
words: hooks and clevises, and all of the things. required to attach

speaker: TW
words: enough horses to a piece of machinery to move it. We weren't bored

speaker: TW
words: in the winter. We got lonely, and we welcomed the chance to meet our

speaker: TW
words: neighbors or a chance to go to town. And a chance to go to town when

speaker: TW
words: the sleighing got good, the sledding or sleighing, as you wish to

speaker: TW
words: call it, this was one of the things that lightened up the chores of

speaker: TW
words: the winter. Always, we managed to get to town at least once sometime

speaker: TW
words: before Christmas each year. Christmas was a pretty big event, just

speaker: TW
words: for this reason, if none other.

speaker: SS
words: I'm curious about this going to town for— were you buying presents?

speaker: TW
words: This would be part of it. Of course, there was supplies, you still

speaker: TW
words: needed supplies that you might have overlooked getting in the sum

speaker: TW
words: mer. You needed kerosene (coal oil) that's what we lighted our hou

speaker: TW
words: ses with. That's what we used when we went to the barn after dark

speaker: TW
words: to care for our animals. Kerosene was the principal ingredient that's

speaker: TW
words: been replaced by electricity. The principal essential ingredient.

speaker: TW
words: We had to have light. And yet, this aspect of farming that you have

speaker: TW
words: shown an interest in— if you'd ask a farmer at that period of the

speaker: TW
words: year, what he was doing, he would have told you, "Nothing." Or, he

speaker: TW
words: might have said,"Just choring around." And, even now, if a farmer

speaker: TW
words: isn't tilling or harvesting or planting or working the land, if you

speaker: TW
words: should ask him what he's doing, he'll say, "Nothing." Occasionally

speaker: TW
words: in the early spring, he might have said, "Fixin' fence." He won't

speaker: TW
words: say that now either.

speaker: SS
words: How close do you feel the connection was with town; the town of

speaker: SS
words: Genesee, for your family? Obviously there was a psychological

speaker: SS
words: as you described to me.

speaker: TW
words: In my lifetime, these towns were almost essential, because they were

speaker: TW
words: the terminal of the railroads. And everything we used, that we couldn't

speaker: TW
words: produce for ourselves, came by way of these railroads. And, I should

speaker: TW
words: go back a little " — anything that was within wagon driving dis

speaker: TW
words: tance, like the wood from Troy, didn't come by railroad, but— the

speaker: TW
words: things I remember getting, of course* we used to vary our diet—

speaker: TW
words: were cheese. We didn't make our cheddar cheese, but every store, any

speaker: TW
words: place you went, had a large, round, flat cylinder of cheese. And the

speaker: TW
words: most interesting mechanism for cutting it and protecting it — and

speaker: TW
words: the cover for protecting it from the flies. And cheese, to anyone of

speaker: TW
words: that day and time meant a slice of this yellow cheddar cheese, of a

speaker: TW
words: wedge shape at one end and a portion of a cylinder at the other. You

speaker: TW
words: still see these wedges of cheese occasionally yet. And if you asked

speaker: TW
words: for a pound of cheese, the grocer made an estimate of what he thought

speaker: TW
words: would be about a pound of cheese and he'd cut it off, and then he

speaker: TW
words: weighed it, and you paid for what he cut. If he cut a way too big,

speaker: TW
words: and you didn't want that much, he would probably have cut an inch off

speaker: TW
words: or another slice off and come a little closer. But usually his guess

speaker: TW
words: was pretty good, and you took it the more or less way that it came

speaker: TW
words: and paid for the quantity you got. Four pounds of cheese, I can visualize

speaker: TW
words: yet. This was a pretty common because you didn t want

speaker: TW
words: to make a trip to town tomorrow to get a slice of cheese. Because a

speaker: TW
words: trip to town tomorrow involved losing a half a day of whatever else

speaker: TW
words: you might have been doing. Another thing I liked about the winter

speaker: TW
words: time; we had no refrigeration remember, so the wintertime was the

speaker: TW
words: time when we butchered. And, there would be butchering day, maybe

speaker: TW
words: more than one. Sometimes neighbors cooperated in these butcherings.

speaker: TW
words: Oftentimes this job was really— because of the people that cooperated

speaker: TW
words: and the companionship that you got, butcherings were a pleasure. They

speaker: TW
words: were an entertainment. They substituted for all of the time-wasting

speaker: TW
words: entertainment that we use now. And, the neighbors would get together

speaker: TW
words: and butcher several hogs or maybe you and your father would butcher

speaker: TW
words: one hog, it varied, depending on your needs and what other people

speaker: TW
words: were doing. And this meat was— if it was hogs you were butcheringv

speaker: TW
words: some of this meat you ate without curing it. But always, you cured

speaker: TW
words: a lot of it. And the hams that we made in those days, part of the cur

speaker: TW
words: ing process resulted from drying out as this salt penetrated the meat.

speaker: TW
words: A ham that you made in those days would last you from the time you

speaker: TW
words: butchered it- if you didn't eat it— would last you from the time you

speaker: TW
words: butchered it until the next winter when you butchered again. This is

speaker: TW
words: a vastly different thing from the ham you buy downtown that is sold

speaker: TW
words: to you with all the water in it that the law permits. And as little

speaker: TW
words: salt in it as your palate permits. The hams and the bacon of my mem

speaker: TW
words: ory, didn't lack for salt. And I'll tell you, by the time the next

speaker: TW
words: butchering came around, you were pretty happy to see some fresh uncured

speaker: TW
words: meat!

speaker: SS
words: Could you tell me, if you're going to go to something else— I'm a

speaker: SS
words: little curious about the butchering get-to-gathers— how that was ar

speaker: SS
words: ranged that a number of neighbors would get together to butcher. How

speaker: SS
words: was that thing set up? Everybody met at one person's place?

speaker: TW
words: No. It was a little different because of the distances involved and

speaker: TW
words: the difficulty in travel. Out butcherings, or least the ones that I

speaker: TW
words: attended, didn't involve the big crowds that might — that I've heard

speaker: TW
words: described where travel was easier and people were closely— neighbors

speaker: TW
words: were closer to each other. But our butchering usually would amount

speaker: TW
words: to a thing like this: One neighbor would say to his neighbor, "I'm

speaker: TW
words: gonna butcher a hog one of these days, why don't you bring something

speaker: TW
words: up and we'll butcher 'em together? And maybe we'll see another neigh

speaker: TW
words: bor and see if he wants to bring one." And so, usually this would con

speaker: TW
words: sist of two, three, or maybe four neighbors. I can't remember a but

speaker: TW
words: chering wher,e we had more than four people involved in butchering.

speaker: TW
words: Families were a unit that we're beginning to forget about now,

speaker: TW
words: but in those days, families a unit. And, if you invited a neigh

speaker: TW
words: bor to come up to your house for dinner, that meant that he was gonna

speaker: TW
words: come and his wife was gonna come and the babies were gonna come, and

speaker: TW
words: the other kids were gonna come, and the hired man would come. It

speaker: TW
words: wasn't even necessary to specifiy this; this was what you meant when

speaker: TW
words: you said come visit us. So, these butcherings, you might have

speaker: TW
words: quite a crowd involving maybe as many as four people— I don't mean

speaker: TW
words: people— four families, and four supplies of meat. Four groups of

speaker: TW
words: animals. You might butcher each one hog. You might butcher each of

speaker: TW
words: you two hogs, or you might butcher as many as, well, say, twelve hogs

speaker: TW
words: at a butchering, or even twenty. And I could go into a little bit of

speaker: TW
words: the butchering.

speaker: SS
words: Why don't you?

speaker: TW
words: Usually the butchering was held at a place where some farmer maybe a

speaker: TW
words: little more enterprising than some of the others, had a vat, in which

speaker: TW
words: to scald the hogs. And this, at our place, consisted of a trough

speaker: TW
words: about a foot and a half deep, with a flat bottom of metal, and sides

speaker: TW
words: and ends of wood, part of which was covered by this metal sheetiron

speaker: TW
words: turned up to protect the lower edges of the wood from the fire which

speaker: TW
words: you built under it. You placed this vat over a ditch that you dug

speaker: TW
words: with the edges of the sides resting on the earth, and the middle part

speaker: TW
words: of the bottom over this ditch. In this ditch you built a fire and

speaker: TW
words: one end of the trough you left the ditch open and at the other end

speaker: TW
words: you put in a stovepipe and closed it up with earth around it, so that

speaker: TW
words: the smoke from this fire went up this pipe and which provided a draft.

speaker: TW
words: And you might have a sheetiron or something that you covered the open

speaker: TW
words: end with and used it to regulate the amount of air, and therefore, the

speaker: TW
words: speed with which the fire burned— the violence with which the fire

speaker: TW
words: burned. And, the man at whose place the butchering was to take place

speaker: TW
words: would probably delegate one of his sons the job of getting this ready

speaker: TW
words: while the father took care of the chores. Or, visa versa; you were

speaker: TW
words: always up early, you didn't get up early for a butchering, you got up

speaker: TW
words: early anyway. You did this preparation instead of some of your other

speaker: TW
words: chores. So, in my time, there was always an old fence post around.

speaker: TW
words: This makes splendid kindling, not only because it burned good, but because

speaker: TW
words: cedar is easy to split, and that's what posts were made of.

speaker: TW
words: A fireplace, or ditch, like this, long kindling and long wood was the

speaker: TW
words: type of thing we burned. So, we heated this water until it got to

speaker: TW
words: the right temperature for scalding a hog. And, the earliest butcher

speaker: TW
words: ings that I went to, you judge the temperature of this water without

speaker: TW
words: a thermometer. An immersion type thermometer was a luxury you didn't

speaker: TW
words: need, because the people that had settled this country if they didn't

speaker: TW
words: know when water was hot, they should have stayed in town! And one

speaker: TW
